content.value: A graph is a set of vertices (nodes) connected by edges. Graphs can be directed or undirected, and weighted or unweighted. They model networks: roads, social connections, dependencies, etc.

items: Adjacency matrix: a V x V grid where cell [i][j] = 1 (or weight) if an edge exists. O(1) edge lookup but O(V^2) space.
Adjacency list: each vertex stores a list of its neighbors. O(V+E) space, better for sparse graphs.

content.value: Explores a graph level by level, visiting all neighbors of a node before moving further out. Uses a queue and a visited array to avoid revisiting nodes. Finds the shortest path (in terms of edge count) in an unweighted graph.
Explores as far as possible down one path before backtracking. Naturally implemented with recursion (which uses the call stack), or iteratively with an explicit stack.

items: BFS: queue-based, finds shortest path in unweighted graphs, uses more memory for wide graphs
DFS: stack/recursion-based, uses less memory for deep graphs, natural for cycle detection and topological sort

content.value: Always mark a node as visited the moment you enqueue/push it, not when you dequeue/pop it - marking too late is the most common bug that causes duplicate visits.

quiz.question: Which data structure does BFS rely on?
Which traversal naturally maps to recursion via the call stack?
Which graph representation is better for sparse graphs?
